_meta.description: 120 scenario-based exam questions for the 40-topic new-grad batch (3 per lesson)

questions.stem: Room 406 (Chen) has a new oxygen requirement (4 L from 2 L) and mild anxiety. Room 407 (Brooks) wants a chaplain visit arranged. Room 410 (Patel) requests a different gown color. Applying "ABCDE Framework for Clinical Prioritization on the Floor", what is your first action?
choices: A. Assess Room 406 (Chen) for work of breathing, lung sounds, and trends
B. Arrange chaplaincy first for Room 407 (Brooks)
C. Change the gown for Room 410 (Patel) to support dignity
D. Offer PRN anxiolytic to Room 406 (Chen) before any physical assessment
questions.rationale: Increasing oxygen needs signal respiratory deterioration risk; assessment precedes medication decisions in "ABCDE Framework for Clinical Prioritization on the Floor".

incorrectRationales.B: Psychosocial support matters but should not precede assessment of worsening oxygenation.
incorrectRationales.C: Gown preference is lowest acuity compared with increased oxygen requirement.
incorrectRationales.D: Medications should follow assessment, not replace it, when respiratory status is changing.

questions.stem: You delegated intake/output recording. The PSW/UAP reports Room 412 (Brooks) has not voided for 10 hours and looks uncomfortable with a distended lower abdomen. Under "ABCDE Framework for Clinical Prioritization on the Floor", what is your priority?
choices: A. Assess the patient yourself and escalate for possible retention/obstruction per findings
B. Tell the PSW/UAP to encourage more oral fluids only
C. Document ‘will monitor’ and defer assessment until rounds
D. Ask the PSW/UAP to insert a urinary catheter independently
questions.rationale: Possible urinary retention requires RN assessment and appropriate orders in "ABCDE Framework for Clinical Prioritization on the Floor". Catheter insertion is not a UAP/PSW function in most settings.

incorrectRationales.B: Fluids alone may worsen retention; assessment comes first.
incorrectRationales.C: Delaying assessment of distension and inability to void is unsafe.
incorrectRationales.D: Invasive procedures require appropriate licensure and orders.

questions.stem: End of shift is 30 minutes away and you have not started handoff notes. A stable patient asks for discharge teaching now. For "ABCDE Framework for Clinical Prioritization on the Floor", what is best?
choices: A. Negotiate a realistic plan: start essential safety teaching now and coordinate with oncoming nurse for completion
B. Rush handoff with missing data to leave on time
C. Promise teaching you cannot complete and leave silently
D. Tell the patient teaching is ‘not nursing’s job’
questions.rationale: Transparent coordination balances teaching continuity with safe handoff in "ABCDE Framework for Clinical Prioritization on the Floor".

incorrectRationales.B: Incomplete handoff risks errors.
incorrectRationales.C: Broken promises and silent departure harm trust and safety.
incorrectRationales.D: Patient education is within nursing scope and unit expectations.

questions.stem: You inherit four patients at 0730 on acute care. Room 407 (Nguyen) is post-op day one with systolic BP 88 mmHg and HR 118 bpm on the last set. Room 408 (Nguyen) is awaiting discharge teaching. Room 410 (Nguyen) asks for a warm blanket. For "Critical Lab Values: Recognizing, Acting, and Documenting Immediately", who do you see first?
choices: A. Room 407 (Nguyen) because hypotension with tachycardia in a fresh post-op patient is a circulation priority
B. Room 408 (Nguyen) because discharge delays frustrate patients and should come first
C. Room 410 (Nguyen) because thermoregulation prevents complications
D. Start med pass for all patients before assessments to stay on time
questions.rationale: In "Critical Lab Values: Recognizing, Acting, and Documenting Immediately", circulation concerns in a post-operative patient (hypotension + tachycardia) outrank discharge logistics and comfort-only requests until stability is clarified.

incorrectRationales.B: Discharge teaching matters but should follow stabilization of possible hemorrhage or hypovolemia.
incorrectRationales.C: Warm blanket is lowest acuity compared with possible post-op hypovolemia or bleeding.
incorrectRationales.D: Medication administration should not precede assessment of a patient with possible hemodynamic compromise.

questions.stem: You delegated a bed bath. The PSW/UAP reports a new stageable pressure injury on the sacrum. Under "Critical Lab Values: Recognizing, Acting, and Documenting Immediately", what should you do first?
choices: A. Assess and measure/document per protocol, notify wound care/provider as required, and update the plan of care
B. Tell the PSW/UAP to apply triple antibiotic ointment they brought from home
C. Ignore it until next shift because baths are low priority
D. Cover it with gauze without assessment to ‘protect dignity’
questions.rationale: New skin breakdown requires RN assessment, documentation, and appropriate referrals in "Critical Lab Values: Recognizing, Acting, and Documenting Immediately".

incorrectRationales.B: Unapproved products and home remedies are unsafe and out of scope.
incorrectRationales.C: Pressure injuries can worsen quickly; delaying assessment is unsafe.
incorrectRationales.D: Dressings without assessment/documentation do not meet standards of care.

questions.stem: Your brain sheet is disorganized after repeated interruptions. For "Critical Lab Values: Recognizing, Acting, and Documenting Immediately", what is the best mid-shift reset?
choices: A. Take 3–5 minutes to rewrite priorities, re-check high-risk tasks, and tell charge if you need redistribution
B. Work faster without a plan
C. Stop assessing patients to reorganize paperwork for 30 minutes
D. Copy another nurse’s brain sheet without understanding their patients
questions.rationale: Brief structured replanning reduces errors during interruption storms in "Critical Lab Values: Recognizing, Acting, and Documenting Immediately".

incorrectRationales.B: Speed without prioritization increases omissions.
incorrectRationales.C: Extended non-clinical pauses can delay necessary assessments.
incorrectRationales.D: Borrowed tools without patient-specific understanding are unsafe.

questions.stem: Room 414 (Patel) is NPO for procedure later today and asks for breakfast now. Room 416 (Patel) has acute abdominal distension and absent bowel sounds with increasing pain. Room 417 (Ibrahim) wants lotion applied. Under "Maslow's Hierarchy Applied to Same-Shift Nursing Priority", which patient needs you first?
choices: A. Room 416 (Patel) because acute abdomen changes can signal obstruction or perforation risk
B. Room 414 (Patel) because NPO patients become hangry and non-adherent
C. Room 417 (Ibrahim) because skin care prevents breakdown
D. Call dietary first to negotiate NPO status for Room 414 (Patel)
questions.rationale: Acute abdomen findings with worsening pain are a safety priority in "Maslow's Hierarchy Applied to Same-Shift Nursing Priority". NPO education and skin care follow after ruling out urgent surgical/medical issues.

incorrectRationales.B: Hunger discomfort does not outrank possible acute abdominal pathology.
incorrectRationales.C: Skin care is important but not before assessment of acute abdomen symptoms.
incorrectRationales.D: Dietary should not be the first stop when another patient has acute abdomen red flags.

questions.stem: You must call the covering provider about Room 413 (Okonkwo) (new hypotension after fluids). Charge is tied up. For "Maslow's Hierarchy Applied to Same-Shift Nursing Priority`, what demonstrates best communication and time management?
choices: A. Collect objective data, use SBAR/ISBAR, state a clear request, and document the interaction per policy
B. Page ‘come now’ without vitals or assessment
C. Wait until 2200 to batch calls
D. Ask the family to page the physician directly
questions.rationale: Prepared structured communication saves time and reduces errors in "Maslow's Hierarchy Applied to Same-Shift Nursing Priority".

incorrectRationales.B: Vague urgent pages delay care and frustrate providers.
incorrectRationales.C: Unstable trends should not wait for convenience batching.
incorrectRationales.D: Families should not replace professional escalation pathways.

questions.stem: The charge nurse asks you to accept a new admission while your current assignment is full. Your other patients are stable except Room 404 (Okonkwo), who has a potassium result just flagged critical low on the monitor and is on active diuretic therapy. For "Medication Priority: Which Meds Cannot Be Late and Why", what should you do first?
choices: A. Go to Room 404 (Okonkwo), assess, and prepare to notify the provider with objective data
B. Accept the admission first because throughput is the unit priority
C. Call the provider about the critical lab before any bedside assessment
D. Ask another nurse to cover your entire assignment while you orient the admission
questions.rationale: Critical values require bedside assessment to pair data with clinical findings before escalation—central to "Medication Priority: Which Meds Cannot Be Late and Why". Accepting a new patient before addressing a critical lab risks harm.

incorrectRationales.B: Unit flow cannot outrank immediate safety actions for a patient with a critical lab on high-risk therapy.
incorrectRationales.C: Calling without assessment gives incomplete information and delays targeted interventions at the bedside.
incorrectRationales.D: Dumping assignment accountability is not an appropriate first step; address the acute safety issue with structured help if needed.

questions.stem: You delegated gathering supplies for a dressing change. The PSW/UAP opened sterile supplies onto a contaminated surface. For "Medication Priority: Which Meds Cannot Be Late and Why", what should you do?
choices: A. Stop the procedure, discard compromised supplies, and redo setup safely with RN oversight/teaching
B. Use the supplies anyway to avoid waste
C. Ask the PSW/UAP to complete the sterile dressing independently
D. Hide the error from the patient to maintain confidence
questions.rationale: Sterility breaches require restart and teaching in "Medication Priority: Which Meds Cannot Be Late and Why" to prevent infection.

incorrectRationales.B: Sterility cannot be compromised to save supplies.
incorrectRationales.C: Sterile technique for complex dressings is typically RN scope; verify policy.
incorrectRationales.D: Concealing errors undermines trust and safety culture.

questions.stem: You are double-booked: physician wants bedside rounds now while your other patient needs a time-sensitive antibiotic. For "Medication Priority: Which Meds Cannot Be Late and Why", what is best?
choices: A. Communicate the time-critical need, coordinate coverage or brief delay with clear patient safety rationale, then execute the priority
B. Skip the antibiotic to look attentive on rounds
C. Hide from the physician until both tasks resolve
D. Delegate antibiotic administration to an unlicensed volunteer
questions.rationale: Negotiation and coverage are appropriate when two legitimate priorities compete in "Medication Priority: Which Meds Cannot Be Late and Why".

incorrectRationales.B: Time-sensitive therapies should not be skipped for appearances.
incorrectRationales.C: Avoidance delays necessary communication.
incorrectRationales.D: Medication administration is not delegated to volunteers.

questions.stem: Room 415 (Ibrahim) reports sudden shortness of breath and SpO2 88% on 2 L nasal cannula (was 94% one hour ago). Room 416 (Hassan) wants pain medication for 4/10 shoulder ache. Room 419 (Patel) needs a signature on a meal tray form. Under "Post-Op vs Chronic Care: Setting Competing Priorities", what is your priority?
choices: A. Assess Room 415 (Ibrahim) immediately for work of breathing and oxygenation
B. Administer PRN analgesia to Room 416 (Hassan) before reassessing oxygen
C. Complete the tray form for Room 419 (Patel) to avoid dietary delays
D. Ask the unit clerk to retake vitals on Room 415 while you finish another task
questions.rationale: Breathing threat (falling oxygen with subjective dyspnea) is the priority in "Post-Op vs Chronic Care: Setting Competing Priorities". The RN should assess and intervene/escalate before lower-acuity comfort or paperwork.

incorrectRationales.B: Mild pain does not precede assessment of acute hypoxemia and increased work of breathing.
incorrectRationales.C: Administrative tasks should not delay assessment of respiratory compromise.
incorrectRationales.D: Delegating reassessment of possible deterioration without RN evaluation is unsafe; the RN should assess first.

questions.stem: Room 406 (Nguyen) reports suicidal ideation with a vague plan (new disclosure). Room 408 (Brooks) wants extra blankets. Room 409 (Rivera) wants the lights dimmed. Under "Prioritizing Multiple Patients: Who Do You See First?", what is your immediate priority?
choices: A. Stay with/ensure safety for Room 406 (Nguyen) and activate crisis/safety protocol per policy
B. Bring blankets first to calm the environment
C. Dim lights for Room 409 (Rivera) to promote sleep
D. Promise confidentiality and leave to get coffee before discussing further
questions.rationale: Safety risk from suicidal ideation is the highest priority in "Prioritizing Multiple Patients: Who Do You See First?" and requires immediate protective actions per institutional protocol.

incorrectRationales.B: Environmental comfort does not precede immediate safety risk.
incorrectRationales.C: Lighting adjustments are not appropriate before addressing suicidal ideation.
incorrectRationales.D: Leaving a patient disclosing SI is unsafe; confidentiality has limits when safety is at risk—follow policy.

questions.stem: You disagree with a telephone order that sounds unclear for high-alert medication dosing. For "Prioritizing Multiple Patients: Who Do You See First?`, what is best?
choices: A. Use read-back, clarify dose/route/frequency, involve second nurse verification if required, and refuse to execute if still ambiguous
B. Guess the dose from handwriting to keep workflow moving
C. Ask a PSW/UAP to confirm the order with the pharmacy
D. Implement the order and chart later
questions.rationale: High-alert medication safety requires clarity and verification in "Prioritizing Multiple Patients: Who Do You See First?".

incorrectRationales.B: Guessing doses is never acceptable.
incorrectRationales.C: Order clarification is a licensed/accountable role per policy.
incorrectRationales.D: Delayed documentation increases error risk.

questions.stem: You delegated ambulation; the PSW/UAP did not report back. You find Room 409 (Brooks) alone in the bathroom, pale, with HR 128 on their wearable. For "Recognizing the Patient Who Cannot Wait: Subtle Cues and Gut Instinct", what reflects best accountability?
choices: A. Assess/treat per protocol, ensure patient safety, then debrief the PSW/UAP on clear reporting expectations
B. Reprimand the PSW/UAP publicly at the nurses’ station first
C. Assume the PSW/UAP is busy and leave the patient in the bathroom
D. Chart that the PSW/UAP was noncompliant without speaking to them
questions.rationale: Patient safety first, then closed-loop feedback—core to "Recognizing the Patient Who Cannot Wait: Subtle Cues and Gut Instinct" after delegation gaps.

incorrectRationales.B: Public shaming undermines teamwork and delays care.
incorrectRationales.C: Leaving a symptomatic patient unattended is unsafe.
incorrectRationales.D: Charting accusations without conversation is unprofessional and may be inaccurate.

questions.stem: During bedside handoff, Room 416 (Ibrahim)’s family interrupts with unrelated stories. For "When Everything Feels Urgent: Triage Thinking on the Medical Floor", what is best?
choices: A. Acknowledge briefly, redirect to patient-centred ISBAR content, and offer a later time for social conversation
B. Stop report entirely until the family finishes
C. Whisper report outside the room without involving the patient
D. Skip allergy verification to save time
questions.rationale: Handoff should stay structured, inclusive of the patient when appropriate, and safety-focused in "When Everything Feels Urgent: Triage Thinking on the Medical Floor".

incorrectRationales.C: Excluding the patient without rationale can reduce engagement and safety checks.

questions.stem: At 1600, Room 411 (Patel) has IV antibiotics due now for sepsis (first dose still not given, admitted 2 hours ago). Room 413 (Okonkwo) wants help rearranging flowers. Room 415 (Brooks) is stable and requests a sleeping mask. For "Documenting Delegation: What the Legal Record Requires", which action comes first?
choices: A. Prepare and administer the time-sensitive antibiotic for Room 411 (Patel) per sepsis urgency
B. Help Room 413 (Okonkwo) with flowers to maintain rapport
C. Bring a mask to Room 415 (Brooks) first because rest aids recovery
D. Batch all tasks until 1700 to improve efficiency
questions.rationale: Time-sensitive sepsis antibiotics are a mortality-linked priority in "Documenting Delegation: What the Legal Record Requires". Non-urgent comfort tasks should follow time-critical therapies when the patient is described as unstable from infection risk.

C. Ask the PSW/UAP to interpret telemetry and adjust antiarrhythmics
D. Ignore the report because the patient walked yesterday without issue
questions.rationale: The RN retains accountability after delegation. New instability symptoms require RN assessment; pushing activity or delegating clinical interpretation is inappropriate in "Documenting Delegation: What the Legal Record Requires".

incorrectRationales.B: Continuing ambulation despite dizziness risks falls and injury.
incorrectRationales.C: Medication and rhythm interpretation are outside UAP/PSW scope.
incorrectRationales.D: Prior tolerance does not negate new symptoms requiring reassessment.

incorrectRationales.B: Snacks are not a safety priority compared with VTE symptoms and insulin infusion accountability.
incorrectRationales.C: Silencing alarms is unsafe; hand off monitoring responsibility instead.
incorrectRationales.D: Leaving without coverage or communication abandons patients and violates accountability.

B. Page ‘come now’ without vitals or assessment

questions.stem: You delegated feeding assistance. The PSW/UAP reports coughing with meals and ‘wet voice’ afterward. For "Following Up After Delegation: Closing the Loop", what is your best next step?

questions.stem: End of shift is 30 minutes away and you have not started handoff notes. A stable patient asks for discharge teaching now. For "Following Up After Delegation: Closing the Loop", what is best?

incorrectRationales.B: Incomplete handoff risks errors.

questions.stem: You promised Room 415 (Hassan) you would return at 1100 for wound teaching. At 1055, Room 413 (Rivera) develops rigors and a temp spike to 39.2°C with HR 118. Room 416 (Ibrahim) calls for a TV remote. For "RN–RPN/LPN Collaboration: What You Can Assign and How", what do you do first?

incorrectRationales.D: Communication should include bedside assessment data, not texts without context (follow unit policy for secure communication).

questions.stem: Room 407 (Nguyen) has acute urinary retention with suprapubic distension and inability to void for 8 hours. Room 409 (Chen) wants a magazine. Room 411 (Chen) asks for a pillow reposition. Applying "ISBAR Handoff: Giving Report That Protects Your Patients", what is your priority?

questions.stem: You are behind on charting but two call bells ring simultaneously. For "ISBAR Handoff: Giving Report That Protects Your Patients`, what reflects safest time management?

questions.stem: You delegated gathering supplies for a dressing change. The PSW/UAP opened sterile supplies onto a contaminated surface. For "Advocating for Your Patient When You Feel Dismissed", what should you do?

questions.stem: You delegated fingerstick blood glucose to the PSW/UAP for Room 405 (Hassan). They report a reading of 2.2 mmol/L and the patient is shaky. For "Escalating Family Concerns to the Interprofessional Team", what should you do first?
